summary: Scientist specializing in translational oncology and drug response biology, combining hands-on bench expertise with computational rigor. Ph.D. in Biology with 5+ years of experience independently designing, executing, and troubleshooting large-scale cell-based and molecular studies — spanning mammalian cell culture, functional assays, and multi-omics platforms — to generate high-quality, reproducible data that has directly informed mechanisms of drug resistance and therapeutic target prioritization. Strong working knowledge of tumor biology and cell signaling pathways (NF-κB, TGF-β), paired with expertise in statistical modeling, multi-omics data integration, and machine learning (R, Python, SQL) to support data-driven decision-making in drug discovery and translational research. Skilled at communicating complex experimental and statistical insights to cross-functional teams; fluent in Mandarin and English.

projects.name: Proteomic Drug-Perturbation Platform for Cancer Drug Resistance
projects.affiliation: Schweppe Lab at Univeristy of Washington
projects.summary: Independently designed and executed a large-scale proteomic drug-perturbation platform across 24 human cancer cell lines and 15 therapeutics, generating and analyzing over 1,000 reproducible proteome and viability profiles. Identified drug-induced network rewiring and protein complex remodeling linking cell signaling and stress-response pathways (NF-κB, proteostasis, TGF-β) to resistance and adaptive drug response, and built predictive models (ElasticNet, LASSO, neural networks) with SHAP-based interpretation to estimate drug sensitivity from molecular features. At the University of Washington's Schweppe Lab.

projects.name: MPK4 Kinase Signaling in Plant Immunity
projects.affiliation: Sixue Chen Lab at Univeristy of Florida
projects.summary: Mapping the MPK4 kinase interactome and phosphorylation dynamics in plant guard cells using TurboID proximity labeling and phosphoproteomics, revealing molecular networks underlying stomatal immune responses in Arabidopsis. Developed and documented standardized protocols (SOPs) to improve reproducibility across experiments. At the University of Florida's Sixue Chen Lab.
